creator: wbe@psr.com
text:
(In reply to Stefan Eßer from comment #9)
> I have tried to build calendar from your tar file on -CURRENT, but have run
> into many compile errors.
Oh?  1) I'm glad to hear you tried.  2) I just used the regular cc and make, nothing special.  3) Since I didn't do this via git, I had to obtain a copy of src.opts.mk to make things work.
The tarball did not include most of ./tests/*.

What sort of errors are you seeing?

> Since this is a mix of bug fixes and improvements, it is hard to review and
> test these individual changes. I had expected a series of patch sets, or at
> least a version that just fixes bugs and then patches that add new features.
The changes weren't small the way one (OK, I) normally thinks of patches.  That's why I submitted them as a tarball.  parsedata.c was renamed parsedate.c.  day.c was eliminated and the useful parts integrated into calendar.c.  calendar.1 was almost completely rewritten. Lots of changes to other files.  I'm not sure patch diffs would make the changes any more readable/understandable than just looking through the new versions.

Check the file CHANGES.  Bugs 1-4 and 11: the description alone should be sufficient to duplicate the fixes in the old code if desired.  Bugs 5-6 + improvement: changed code to remove trailing '.' from tz/locale abbreviations.  Also, changed code to support UTF-8 encoding in dates and tz/locale names, which it previously didn't.  That was a more extensive change, but now it supports case-independent matching of non-ASCII names.  See CHANGES for more on this.  Bugs 7-9 (Chinese New Year): the whole buggy thing was eliminated and replaced with a paragraph or two elsewhere.

> (assuming that not all these changes have been worked on simultanously).
They kind of were.  The bug fixes happened as a side-effect of making the main change I wanted to make which was to support floating dates with an offset; e.g., SepFirstMon+6 (the first Sunday after the first Monday in September, which is unofficially Grandparent's Day in the U.S.).  That required changing date parsing and interpretation in non-trivial ways, and it turned out to be easier and better to just redo the whole parser/interpreter and event handler interaction in a clearer and more general way that allows date specs in a much wider range of formats.  Things progressed from there to other useful changes/additions.

IMHO, the new code is pretty readable.  Rather than look at each change separately, look at the new code and man calendar.1.  For the files that weren't extensively changed, diff -b.  The 2 patches to the tarball were to adopt changes from other bugzilla bug reports.

If I can help, let me know.
